title: The Empty Interface
description: Handling any data type in Go.

slides.title: The Ultimate Wildcard 🃏
slides.content:
## Accepting `interface{}`

What if you want to write a function that genuinely accepts *any* piece of data in the world? An `int`, a `string`, a custom `User` struct?

You can use the **Empty Interface**: `interface{}`.

Because it requires *zero* methods, literally every single data type in Go automatically fulfills it!

slides.title: Print Anything 🖨️
slides.content: This is exactly how `fmt.Println` works behind the scenes! It accepts the empty interface, which is why you can throw numbers, strings, and structs into it without it crashing.

quizData.question: Why does every single data type in Go flawlessly automatically fulfill the 'Empty Interface' `interface{}`?

options.text: Because it actively demands exactly zero methods.
It magically converts everything to strings.
Because Go is a dynamic language.

quizData.explanation: Since fulfillment purely depends on possessing required methods, demanding completely zero seamlessly perfectly ensures physically explicitly cleanly perfectly everything solidly matches flawlessly.

slides.title: A Word of Caution ⚠️
slides.content:
## Use Sparingly

While treating everything as `interface{}` is powerful, it completely destroys Go's famous Type Safety. If you pass a string where you meant to pass an integer, the compiler can't warn you anymore.

Only use empty interfaces when you truly don't care what data type arrives.
